explicit_text: I am CeeCee Ross Lyles, originally from Fort Pierce, Florida. After six years working as a police officer and detective there, I transitioned to a career as a flight attendant with United Airlines, completing my training in January 2001. I was a busy mother of two sons and two stepsons, and I loved the travel opportunities my new job provided, as well as the time I could spend at home between flights. During the tragic hijacking of Flight 93, I called my husband twice, sharing my love and faith in those final moments.

explicit_text: The United States National Park Service is the official government organization that manages the Flight 93 National Memorial. They provide resources and information about the events surrounding Flight 93 and honor the passengers and crew who showed incredible courage. Their website and app help visitors explore and understand the significance of this historic site. I have seen how they preserve the memory of Flight 93 and support families affected by the tragedy.

explicit_text: Flight 93 was the flight I was working on as a United Airlines flight attendant in September 2001. During the hijacking, I made two calls to my husband, expressing my love and faith as we faced the crisis. The passengers and crew, including myself, showed tremendous bravery in trying to regain control of the plane. The flight tragically crashed, but our actions prevented further devastation. Flight 93 remains a symbol of courage and sacrifice.

It’s where I raised my family and built a strong foundation before embarking on my new career with United Airlines. My experiences there shaped who I am and gave me the strength and resilience I needed during the events of Flight 93. Fort Pierce will always be an important part of my story.

My role as a flight attendant was to care for passengers, and in those final moments, I reached out to my husband to share my love and faith.
